intro:
The Temptation of Christ is narrated in Matthew 4:1-11 and Luke 4:1-13, with a brief reference in Mark 1:12-13. Immediately after his baptism and the divine declaration of sonship, Jesus was 'led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil' (Matt. 4:1). The forty days echo Israel's forty years in the wilderness, and each of Satan's three temptations presupposes a distorted use of divine power, countered by Jesus with quotations from Deuteronomy. The sequence in Matthew: (1) turning stones to bread — provision apart from the Father's timing, countered with Deut. 8:3: 'man does not live on bread alone'; (2) casting himself from the pinnacle of the temple — presumptuous testing of divine protection, Satan citing Ps. 91:11-12, countered with Deut. 6:16: 'do not put the LORD your God to the test'; (3) worshipping Satan in exchange for the kingdoms of the world — idolatry for a shortcut to messianic rule, countered with Deut. 6:13: 'worship the LORD your God, and serve him only.'

The temptation narrative is theologically important in multiple respects. It demonstrates that Jesus was genuinely tempted as a human being — the author of Hebrews insists that he 'has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin' (Heb. 4:15; cf. 2:18), making his sympathetic high priesthood dependent on this genuine human experience of testing. The typological comparison with Israel is explicit: where Israel failed God's tests in the wilderness (grumbling for bread, testing God at Massah, worshipping the golden calf), Jesus as the obedient Israel succeeds — not by extraordinary divine power but by faithful use of Scripture and submission to the Father's will. Luke's note that the devil departed 'until an opportune time' (4:13) indicates that temptation continued throughout Jesus's ministry, reaching its climax in Gethsemane (Luke 22:28).
